topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the dusty attic floor, a cardboard tomb filled with the ghosts of forgotten schoolwork. Liam stared at it, his jaw tight. His mother had always been meticulous, organized to the point of obsession, and now this—a veritable mountain of graded papers, neatly stacked by subject, dated and filed. A low, simmering resentment began to bubble in his chest. He picked up a crumpled essay, the ink faded, and felt a sharp, unpleasant twist in his gut. "Why?" he muttered to the empty room, his voice rough. He shoved the box back down, the sound of the lid thudding echoing in the silence. He didn't want to look at any of it.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya scoffed, her eyes narrowing as she surveyed the overflowing filing cabinet in the spare room. Every textbook, every worksheet, every test from the last decade was cataloged. A wave of intense irritation washed over her, making her shoulders tense up beneath her worn t-shirt. She ran a hand through her hair, the gesture jerky and agitated. "She kept *everything*?" she questioned, her tone sharp, laced with disbelief. She felt a deep sense of being undermined, as if this collection was a deliberate, passive-aggressive statement. She kicked lightly at a stack of geometry proofs, the movement sharp and dismissive.

[story 3]

I found the storage bin tucked behind the seldom-used bookshelf. It was overflowing, a chaotic landscape of blue folders and yellowed notebooks. A cold knot formed in my stomach as I approached it. Every single assignment, every graded paper from elementary school through high school, was present. I didn't feel curiosity; I felt a profound sense of being judged. My hands trembled slightly as I lifted a textbook, the weight of the paper pressing down on my palms. "What is this supposed to be?" I demanded, the question coming out clipped and brittle. I wanted nothing more than for this entire situation to vanish.
